text: This video describes the technique of laparoscopic assisted left hemicolectomy with superior rectal artery preservation. This is a sixty-eight year old woman with hematasia and abdominal pain. Preoperative staging by CT reveals a stage three colon cancer at the descending sigmoid junction. This graph shows the resection range. This graph shows the choke-up placement.

This video describes the technique of laparoscopic assisted left hemicolectomy with superior rectal artery preservation. This is a sixty-eight year old woman with hematasia and abdominal pain. Preoperative staging by CT reveals a stage three colon cancer at the descending sigmoid junction. This graph shows the resection range. This graph shows the choke-up placement.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic assisted left hemicolectomy with a medium approach, identifying and protecting the superior hypogastric plexus. Central lymph nodes around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) are carefully removed.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic assisted left hemicolectomy with a medium approach, identifying and protecting the superior hypogastric plexus. Central lymph nodes around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) are removed.

The surgeon isolates and cuts the left colic artery at its origin while retaining the superior rectal artery. The hypogastric nerves are identified and protected. Dissection of the mesocolon proceeds from median to lateral along the left torus line. The left ureter is identified and protected, and the root of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is ligated and cut.

The surgeon isolates and cuts the left colic artery at its origin while retaining the superior rectal artery. The hypogastric nerves are identified and protected. Dissection of the mesocolon proceeds from median to lateral along the left torus line. The left ureter is identified and protected, and the root of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is ligated and cut.

The surgeon performs granular dissection of the mesocolon up to the upper border of the pancreas. The sigmoid colon is mobilized laterally, distally towards the rectum, and proximally towards the descending colon.

The surgeon continues the granular dissection of the mesocolon up to the upper border of the pancreas. The sigmoid colon is mobilized laterally, distally towards the rectum, and proximally towards the descending colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, confirming the complete mobilization of the sigmoid and descending colon. The specimen is removed through a small incision, followed by extracorporeal anastomosis.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, confirming the complete mobilization of the sigmoid and descending colon. The specimen is extracted through a small incision, followed by extracorporeal anastomosis.
